topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery arrived with a soft thud on the doorstep. A vibrant bouquet of sunflowers, their heads heavy with pollen, sat amidst the usual clutter of the entryway. I blinked slowly, my eyelids feeling weighted with inertia. A bunch of cheerful, utterly unsolicited flora. I didn't move from the sofa, just let my gaze drift over the bright yellows and deep browns. Why bother? Another thing to manage. I let a faint sigh escape, the sound barely disturbing the quiet hum of the afternoon. I nudged the nearest cushion with my toe, deciding that perhaps tomorrow would be a better time to consider what to do with this unexpected decoration.

[story 2]

When the doorbell rang, I didn't immediately rise. My feet remained planted firmly on the worn rug, a deep sense of resistance radiating from my shoulders. I shuffled toward the door, moving with the slow deliberation of someone wading through thick syrup. The box was handed over, and inside, the arrangement of roses was undeniably beautiful, a splash of intense color against the muted tones of my living room. I took the flowers, letting them feel cool against my palms. I looked at the empty space where a card should have been, a momentary flicker of mild annoyance crossing my features before it was smoothed away by sheer apathy. I tucked the stems haphazardly into a nearby vase, planning on ignoring them until the next major life event.

[story 3]

The package sat by the mail slot, an intrusion of bright color in the otherwise monochrome landscape of my morning. I peered at it, a flicker of mild curiosity battling the overwhelming urge to remain exactly where I was—sunk deep into the soft embrace of my armchair. The flowers were undeniably pretty, a sudden burst of visual stimulation that felt entirely unnecessary. I let my eyes linger for a moment, tracing the velvety texture of the petals. A low murmur escaped my lips, a sound more of resignation than greeting. With a deliberate, almost sluggish movement, I picked them up, the weight negligible in my hands. They were just… things. I placed them on the nearest flat surface, resolved to deal with them only when the sheer effort of moving them became too much to bear.
